blocks.text: Interactive components attach an `action`. Two kinds: a **server action** (`event`, sent over the network) or a **local action** (`functionCall`, executed entirely on the client).

cards.back: Client → Server. A user interacted with a component that has a server action. Carries sourceComponentId, context, and optionally wantResponse + actionId.
Server → Client. Answers an action that set wantResponse: true, matched by actionId. Exactly one of value or error.
Server → Client. Ask the client to run a function IT has registered — never raw code sent over the wire. Carries functionCallId.
Client → Server. The successful result of a callFunction, keyed by functionCallId. A FAILED call goes back as a separate error message instead — functionResponse itself carries no error field.

blocks.text: Execution boundaries — the real security mechanism

Every catalog function declares `callableFrom`: `clientOnly`, `remoteOnly`, or `clientOrRemote` (defaults to `clientOnly` if omitted). When a server sends `callFunction` for a name the client's catalog marks `clientOnly` — or a name it doesn't recognize at all — the client MUST refuse and reply with a client-to-server `error`, code `INVALID_FUNCTION_CALL`. This is verified at runtime against the live catalog, not validated on the wire.

tabs.label: Rejected call

blocks.title: Why this matters
blocks.text: This is the concrete mechanism behind A2UI's headline security claim: an agent can request behavior, never inject behavior. Functions are named, catalog-registered, and boundary-checked — the agent supplies a name and arguments, never a script body.

Input components (and Buttons) can declare `checks` — each a function call that must return a boolean. A failing check shows its message; on a Button, a failing check disables it entirely, letting a button's enabled state depend on live form validity.

tabs.label: Field validation
tabs.code:
"checks": [
  {"call": "required",
   "args": {"value": {"path": "/formData/zip"}},
   "message": "Zip code is required"},
  {"call": "regex",
   "args": {"value": {"path": "/formData/zip"}, "pattern": "^[0-9]{5}$"},
   "message": "Must be a 5-digit zip code"}
]

blocks.text: How agents actually generate valid A2UI

The spec names a specific authoring loop for LLM-driven generation: **prompt → generate → validate**. The prompt includes the target UI description, the A2UI schema (including the active catalog), and example payloads. The LLM generates JSON. It's validated against the schema; failures are reported back to the LLM in the standard `VALIDATION_FAILED` shape (`code`, `surfaceId`, `path`, `message`) so it can self-correct in the next turn.

blocks.question: A server sends callFunction for a function the client's catalog doesn't recognize at all. What must the client do?
options: Execute it anyway if the args look safe
Reject it and send back an error with code INVALID_FUNCTION_CALL
Ask the user for permission first
blocks.explanation: Unrecognized functions are treated the same as clientOnly functions called remotely — immediate rejection, INVALID_FUNCTION_CALL. No code ever runs that wasn't already registered and boundary-checked.

points: callFunction/functionResponse is server-initiated RPC — the client runs code it already registered, never code sent over the wire
callableFrom (clientOnly/remoteOnly/clientOrRemote) is the real, runtime-enforced security boundary
checks give components live validation and can gate a Button's enabled state
Generation is a loop: prompt with schema+examples -> LLM generates -> validate -> report errors back for self-correction
